Point number five on our list is reason why. Reason why advertising, you guys all know about it, and it's important for a lot of things. When you present opportunity, it's even more important because there are profound causes for skepticism about opportunity. The biggest of which is if it's so good, why share it? Why don't you just do it? I have a friend right now who absolutely refuses to buy gold and is absolutely a cynic about all of the advertising selling gold. Here's his logic. Here's why he would never buy gold. You ready? Because if the shit was so valuable, why would they be selling it? And you cannot disabuse him of this idea. Of course, everything of value is sold by somebody. On that argument, nobody would ever sell anything. Everybody would be buying. But that's where he is. Everybody would go, because if the stuff was so valuable, why would anybody sell it? They would just keep it. Why would they turn it into paper? The whole argument for buying it is the argument why they shouldn't be selling it. The same thing exists many times with opportunity. If you really have a system that works for taking shoe stores and making them ten times more valuable than the average shoe store, why in the hell don't you just open up a bunch of shoe stores? And it's actually a pretty logical objection. It makes some sense. If you really have a way to flood chiropractic offices with patients, why wouldn't you just own a bunch of chiropractic offices? So that's one of a number of big questions in their mind that require a good reason why. Can't be ignored has to be answered. You'll see I gave you a very basic example, and it's only interesting as trivia, because it actually comes from Chase Ravel, who created Entrepreneur Magazine, although it says John Ravel here, but same guy. But in my Jeff Paul ad, so that ad, which many of you will remember, there is, we use it right up front, so your subhead across the top says, why would any sane person reveal this kind of secret if it was true? And then in the center column, about here, there is, why would I share all this with you? So we deal with it and actually make it somewhat of a focal point of the ad itself. And this ad also has a Blackie story, by the way, just as an aside. So how many know what a Blackie story is? Oh, good for you. For the rest of you, very quickly, a Blackie story is, it's linked to reason why. The Blackie story is always about the person who opened the door for now the teller of the story and the seller of the opportunity. So it could be a dying man with his final breath. It could be the billionaire who is finally ready to retire and disappear and has revealed his secret to him. But there is this other person who has, and often the dying man thing is the original Blackie story. But there is always another figure from who was the source of the secrets, the methods, the strategies, so that the seller is really moving legacy rather than inventing. Why do you think a Blackie story works so well? Well, social proof, it brings two things to the table. One, it actually brings a reason why. So the dying man with his last breath shared it with me, so I am now compelled to share it with you. Now it doesn't actually, because really, if it was so good and you were prone to keep it, it wouldn't matter who they all gave it to you, you would keep it, right? So it really doesn't, but it sounds like it answers the reason why. The dying man shared it with me, right? My great grandfather passed it to my grandfather, my grandfather passed it to me, I feel obligated to share. So it answers the reason why. Secondly, it makes the source not the seller, which automatically makes it more believable. So if I thought it up, like your Russian guy is a Blackie story, John, which you probably know, that's what it is, right? It's much better than if you invented the kettlebell. Much better that this guy is from Russian crawled under the barbed wire, and it's a much better story. So the Blackie story, if we just thought it up, and we say, I'm sitting in my basement one day, what did George say about the thing? Well, I thought it up while I was riding my horse through the mountains in Colorado. Well, that doesn't sound real credible. I mean, that doesn't give me a real sense of reassurance and confidence in this thing, right? But if he got it from a grizzled old mail order veteran who was hiding and spending his retirement years in the mountains of Colorado, and one night over a bottle of whiskey by the campfire, the guy broke down and shared it with him with the commitment that George would carefully share it with other ambitious... See, that's a much better story, and it makes the thing more credible. It really has nothing to do with the validity of the thing, but it makes it sound a lot more credible. So reason why an opportunity is extremely important. One clever reason why that is often used, and I wanted to show you a great example of it, is timing. So I'm going to show you one example that's in your book, but this is used over and over and over again.
